hook: Art Deco family caff on Byres Road, barely changed since the 1930s and run by the Verrecchias since 1918.
facts: Opened in 1918 and has been run by the Verrecchia family ever since, now into a fourth generation.
The Verrecchias came from the Cassino area of Italy, part of the wave of Italians who settled in Scotland from the mid-19th century.
The interior retains its original Art Deco fittings and wooden booths, little changed since the 1930s.
Known for fish suppers, fry-ups and award-winning Italian ice cream made on the premises.
Address is 87 Byres Road, Glasgow G11 5HN.
legend: Sometimes claimed locally as a contender for the home of the original deep-fried Mars bar.
Has drawn celebrity fans including chefs Rick Stein and the late Anthony Bourdain.

sources.note: Detailed history of the Verrecchia family ownership from 1918 and the Italian-Scots context.
Describes the 1930s Art Deco interior, menu and family ownership.

w: Run by the Verrecchia family since 1918 and barely touched since the 1930s — original Art Deco fittings, wooden booths, and ice cream still churned on the spot on Byres Road. Fish suppers, fry-ups, and a fourth generation behind the counter. Anthony Bourdain and Rick Stein both made the pilgrimage. Unchanged on purpose.

hook: The St Vincent Street club where Alan McGee saw Oasis and signed them on the spot in 1993.
facts: Opened in February 1990 in the former Saints and Sinners pub by DF Concerts founder Stuart Clumpas.
Named after a 1980s New York club of the same name.
On 31 May 1993 Oasis played here and were signed to Creation Records by Alan McGee, who happened to be in the audience.
Within a two-week stretch in 1993 The Verve, Radiohead and Oasis all played the venue.
Address is 272a St Vincent Street, Glasgow G2 5RL; Wikipedia gives coordinates 55deg51'47"N 4deg15'54"W.
legend: The Oasis signing is one of the most retold origin stories in British indie rock and cemented King Tut's reputation as a talent-spotting venue.
Acts including Manic Street Preachers, Teenage Fanclub, Primal Scream and Snow Patrol have played its small upstairs stage.

sources.note: Founding date, name origin, Oasis/Creation signing and coordinates 55.8631, -4.2650.
Account of the 31 May 1993 Oasis gig and McGee signing.

w: The St Vincent Street club where Alan McGee, in the crowd by chance on 31 May 1993, watched Oasis and signed them to Creation on the spot. In one fortnight that year The Verve, Radiohead and Oasis all played the little upstairs room. Open since 1990, still a talent-spotter's stage. Where Britpop got its break.

hook: Fiercely independent record shop tucked in a railway arch inside the vegan cafe-bar Mono, co-founded by Stephen Pastel.
facts: Opened in 2002 inside Mono cafe-bar at 12 King's Court, off King Street, Merchant City (G1 5RB).
Co-founded by Stephen McRobbie (Stephen Pastel) of the cult Glasgow indie band The Pastels.
Devoted to vinyl, with a deep range of independent, experimental and reissue stock.
Sits in a railway-arch space symbiotically attached to Mono, which doubles as a gig venue.
King Street in the Merchant City geocodes to roughly 55.855, -4.247 (G1 5RB end).
legend: Regularly cited as one of the UK's best independent record shops and a hub of Glasgow's alternative music community.

sources.note: Confirms 2002 opening, Stephen Pastel connection and the Mono / railway-arch setting.
King Street G1 5RB (Mono's postcode) geocodes to ~55.8552, -4.2472 (coordinate corroboration).

w: A fiercely independent record shop in a railway arch, folded into the vegan cafe-bar Mono off King Street — co-founded in 2002 by Stephen McRobbie of The Pastels. Deep in independent, experimental and reissue vinyl, with a gig space next door. One of the UK's best, and a hub of Glasgow's alternative scene. Dig, then stay for a pint.

hook: Glasgow's beloved arthouse cinema in a windowless 1939 Dutch-modernist building, once the Cosmo.
facts: The building opened on 18 May 1939 as the Cosmo, Scotland's first arthouse cinema and only the second purpose-built arthouse in Britain after the Curzon Mayfair.
It was the last cinema built in Glasgow before the outbreak of the Second World War.
Designed by James McKissack and W J Anderson II, its brick, windowless facade was influenced by Dutch modernist Willem Dudok.
Reopened as the Glasgow Film Theatre under the Scottish Film Council on 2 May 1974.
Category B listed; address 12 Rose Street, Glasgow; Wikipedia coordinates 55.86575, -4.26122.

sources.note: 1939 Cosmo opening, Dudok-influenced design, 1974 GFT reopening, listing status and coordinates 55.86575, -4.26122.
GFT's own history confirming the Cosmo origins and arthouse status.

w: Scotland's first arthouse cinema, opened in 1939 as the Cosmo behind a brick, windowless facade lifted from the Dutch modernist Dudok — the last picture house built in Glasgow before the war. It became the GFT under the Scottish Film Council in 1974, and remains the city's home of world and independent film. The Cosmo, by another name.

hook: Gloriously chaotic second-hand bookshop in a bohemian West End lane, where you dig through teetering piles to find treasure.
facts: Located at 12-14 Otago Lane, a tiny lane in Hillhead near Kelvingrove Park and the University of Glasgow.
A family-run second-hand and antiquarian bookshop, long operated by two brothers.
Famous for its cluttered, floor-to-ceiling stacks, with books piled in the aisles as well as on shelves.
Despite the chaos, stock is roughly arranged by subject and covers almost every topic, including long out-of-print titles.
Address 12-14 Otago Lane geocodes to 55.8728, -4.2822.
legend: Routinely named one of Glasgow's best bookshops and a favourite photographic subject for its overflowing, ramshackle interior.

sources.note: Describes the chaotic stacks, family ownership and Otago Lane location.

w: A second-hand bookshop so gloriously overrun that books are stacked in the aisles as well as floor to ceiling — you climb the piles to find the treasure. Down bohemian Otago Lane near Kelvingrove, family-run by two brothers, it's one of Glasgow's most photographed interiors. Chaos, loosely sorted by subject. Bring time and dust tolerance.

hook: A wrought-iron Victorian glasshouse, floated up the Clyde by barge, that crowns the West End's Botanic Gardens.
facts: The Glasgow Botanic Gardens cover over 20 hectares in the West End and hold more than 9,000 plant species.
The Kibble Palace was originally built around 1860 as a conservatory for John Kibble's estate on Loch Long.
In 1871-73 it was dismantled, floated by barge down the Clyde and reconstructed in the Botanic Gardens, opening to the public in 1873.
The curved iron-and-glass structure covers about 2,137 square metres and was originally lit by some 600 gas lamps.
It houses the national collection of tree ferns; gardens coordinates 55.87930, -4.29066.
legend: The barge journey of the great glasshouse down Loch Long and up the Clyde is a celebrated piece of Glasgow engineering folklore.
In its early years the lit palace was used for grand concerts and gatherings, including political meetings.

sources.note: Gardens history, Kibble Palace relocation by barge, opening 1873 and coordinates 55.87930, -4.29066.
Friends of the Gardens history confirming the structure's origins, dimensions and relocation.

w: A wrought-iron Victorian glasshouse from John Kibble's Loch Long estate, dismantled and floated down the Clyde by barge to be rebuilt in the Botanic Gardens in 1873. Curved iron and glass, once lit by 600 gas lamps, now sheltering the national tree-fern collection. The West End's crystal palace.

hook: The curry-tapas restaurant opposite Kelvingrove that turned Indian sharing plates into a Glasgow institution.
facts: Opened in 2004 at 1355 Argyle Street, directly across from Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum.
Its signature format is curry served tapas-style, with around 40 small dishes to mix and share rather than a few large mains.
It was the first expansion of the Mother India family of restaurants, which grew to several Glasgow outlets and one in Edinburgh.
Decor evokes colonial-era Indian dining rooms with retro furnishings and black-and-white photographs.
Address 1355 Argyle Street (G3 8AD) geocodes to 55.8673, -4.2911.
legend: Widely credited with popularising the curry-tapas small-plates style now copied across the city.

sources.note: History as a Glasgow institution, 2004-era opening, the curry-tapas concept and decor.

w: The place opposite Kelvingrove that turned curry into tapas — forty small dishes to mix and share instead of one big plate, a format the whole city soon copied. A 2004 spin-off of the Mother India family, in retro colonial-dining-room surroundings. Sharing plates before sharing plates were everywhere. Order too much; you'll want to.

hook: Tiny Italian-style espresso bar just off Buchanan Street that helped set the bar for Glasgow's specialty coffee scene.
facts: Opened in autumn 2013 at 93 West Nile Street, just behind Buchanan Street.
Designed around Italian espresso culture combined with modern specialty-coffee technique.
Pulls shots on a La Marzocco machine and rotates beans from small-batch roasters.
The compact ten-seat interior was shortlisted for the Architects' Journal Small Projects Award 2014; its ceiling uses timber reclaimed from the Glasgow School of Art.
Address 93 West Nile Street geocodes to 55.8632, -4.2548.
legend: Frequently named among the best coffee bars in Glasgow and credited with raising the city's espresso standards.

sources.note: 2013 opening, Italian-espresso concept, La Marzocco, reclaimed GSA timber and design-award shortlisting.
Geocodes 93 West Nile Street to 55.8631864, -4.2547772 (coordinate corroboration).

w: A ten-seat Italian espresso bar off Buchanan Street that helped set Glasgow's coffee standard when it opened in 2013 — La Marzocco shots, rotating small-batch beans, and a ceiling built from timber salvaged from the Glasgow School of Art. The little room was even shortlisted for an architecture award. Stand-up espresso, done right.
